basic_info.name: Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC)
basic_info.description: Environmental Stress Cracking occurs when a stressed polymer part is exposed to a chemical agent that would not cause failure in the absence of stress. HSP-based RED values between the polymer and the chemical environment predict ESC risk: chemicals with RED values near or below 1.0 (partial solubility) are the most dangerous ESC agents, as they can absorb into the stressed polymer surface and promote craze-to-crack transitions. This approach is critical for material selection in chemical environments, enabling rapid screening of polymer-chemical combinations for ESC susceptibility without lengthy mechanical testing.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: ESC is driven by localized plasticization and chain disentanglement at crack tips where absorbed solvent molecules reduce the local Tg and craze fibril stability. The HSP distance between polymer and environmental fluid determines the degree of absorption and plasticization. Critically, ESC does not require full dissolution (RED < 1.0); chemicals in the RED range of approximately 0.7-1.3 are often the most dangerous because they absorb enough to plasticize without fully dissolving the polymer. The mechanism involves: (1) stress-enhanced absorption at craze tips due to dilatation and increased free volume, (2) local reduction in yield stress and craze fibril strength, and (3) accelerated craze-to-crack transition. The RED threshold for ESC onset is typically lower than for bulk dissolution because mechanical stress effectively enlarges the solubility sphere. The delta_H (hydrogen bonding) component is particularly important for predicting ESC in polar polymers.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD1-dD2)^2 + (dP1-dP2)^2 + (dH1-dH2)^2); RED = Ra/R0; ESC risk is highest when 0.7 < RED < 1.3
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED < 0.8 high ESC risk (also likely full dissolution); RED 0.8-1.0 significant ESC risk; RED 1.0-1.3 moderate ESC risk under high stress; RED > 1.3 low ESC risk

required_inputs: HSP values (dD, dP, dH) for the polymer
Interaction radius R0 for the polymer
HSP values (dD, dP, dH) for the environmental chemical/fluid
Applied stress level (or strain) on the polymer part
data_sources: Material supplier technical datasheets (polymer ESC resistance ratings)
ASTM D1693 (ESCR bent strip test data for polyethylene)
Literature compilations of ESC agent rankings
Chemical product SDS sheets (for complex mixture HSP estimation)
complementary_parameters: Polymer molecular weight and molecular weight distribution
Residual stress from processing (injection molding, thermoforming)
Polymer orientation and weld line locations

limitations: ESC is a kinetic/mechanical phenomenon; HSP provides thermodynamic screening but cannot predict time-to-failure quantitatively
Stress level strongly modulates the effective R0 (stressed polymer has larger effective solubility sphere), but this stress-modified R0 is not well-characterized for most polymers
Complex chemical mixtures (detergents, oils, cleaning agents) require HSP estimation of the mixture, introducing additional uncertainty
Polymer processing history (residual stress, orientation, crystallinity) dramatically affects ESC resistance but is not captured by HSP
Does not distinguish between slow crack growth and rapid brittle failure modes
Surface treatments, coatings, and fillers modify ESC behavior beyond what HSP predicts
known_failure_modes: Polycarbonate ESC with certain aromatic solvents occurs at RED values somewhat above 1.0 due to specific aromatic-aromatic interactions not fully captured by HSP
Surfactant-induced ESC in polyethylene: aqueous surfactant solutions have high RED but still cause ESC by surface wetting and reducing surface energy at crack tips
Silicone fluids causing ESC in some elastomers despite seemingly high RED values
Mixtures where minor component with low RED drives ESC despite high overall mixture RED
validation_method: ASTM D1693 bent strip test (for PE)
ASTM F2136 notched constant tensile load (NCTL) test
Slow crack growth testing (ASTM F1473 PENT test)
Full notch creep test (FNCT) per ISO 16770
Strain-rate controlled tensile testing in chemical environment
Bergen jig constant strain ESC test

key_references: Hansen, C.M.; Just, L. 'Prediction of Environmental Stress Cracking in Plastics with Hansen Solubility Parameters', Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research, 2001, 40(1), 21-25
Wright, D.C. 'Environmental Stress Cracking of Plastics', Rapra Technology, 1996
Hansen, C.M. 'Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook', 2nd Ed., CRC Press, 2007, Chapter 18
Arnold, J.C. 'The influence of liquid uptake on environmental stress cracking of glassy polymers', Materials Science and Engineering A, 2004, 384(1-2), 249-256

Soares, J.B.P.; Abbott, R.F.; Kim, J.D. 'Environmental stress cracking resistance of polyethylene: the use of CRYSTAF and SEC to establish structure-property relationships', Journal of Polymer Science Part B, 2000, 38(10), 1267-1275
literature.regulatory_relevance: ESC testing is required for plastic piping (ASTM D1693, ISO 16770) in water/gas distribution standards; chemical resistance data including ESC is required for ATEX-rated plastic enclosures; FDA food-contact polymer parts must demonstrate resistance to ESC from cleaning agents

industry_sectors: Automotive (fuel system components, under-hood parts exposed to fluids)
Packaging (HDPE containers for detergents, chemicals, agricultural products)
Medical devices (polymer components exposed to disinfectants and cleaning agents)
commercial_tools: HSPiP (ESC screening module with chemical-polymer RED mapping)
CAMPUS plastics database (ESC ratings for commercial grades)
UL Prospector (chemical resistance data for plastics)
Matweb (material property database with ESC resistance data)
